title: Agentic AI Frameworks: Building and Deploying Intelligent Agents
description: A hands-on intermediate course for software developers and AI/ML engineers covering how LLM-powered agents work, how to build them using popular frameworks like LangGraph, and how to select the right framework for real-world use cases.

lessons.title: What Are AI Agents? Core Concepts and Architecture
lessons.content:
# What Are AI Agents? Core Concepts and Architecture

Most LLM apps are **one-shot**: you send a prompt, you get text back.

An **AI agent** is what happens when you wrap an LLM in a **control loop** so it can decide *what to do next*—including calling tools, checking results, updating memory, and repeating until the goal is actually done.

![LLM-powered autonomous agent system overview (planning, memory, tools). (Weng, 2023)](/api/wiki-images/agent-fundamentals/images/lilianweng-posts-2023-06-23-agent_001.png)

## The defining difference: who controls the next step?

A plain LLM call is like asking a smart intern for a paragraph.

An agent is like giving that intern:
- a checklist of available **tools** (APIs, databases, code execution),
- a notebook (**memory**),
- and permission to keep working until the task is complete.

The key property is **autonomous decision-making**: the system (via the LLM) chooses whether to think more, use a tool, ask a clarifying question, or stop.

## The agent loop: observe → plan → act → update

Nearly every agent architecture can be understood as variations on this loop:

1. **Observe**: read the user request + current state + tool outputs.
2. **Plan**: decide the next step (or a short sequence of steps).
3. **Act**: call a tool (or produce a final answer).
4. **Update memory/state**: store what matters; trim what doesn’t.
5. Repeat until a **stop condition** is met (done, failed safely, or hit limits).

This is the “agent function” idea in modern clothing: map the history of perceptions (messages, tool results) to the next action.

## Tools: how agents touch the real world

LLMs don’t *actually* query your database or send an email. Agents do—by letting the model emit a structured **tool call** that your code executes.

Here’s the minimal pattern:

```python
# Pseudocode: the classic tool-calling loop
while True:
    msg = model(prompt, tools=tool_schemas, state=state)

    if msg.type == "tool_call":
        result = run_tool(msg.name, msg.args)
        state.messages.append({"role": "tool", "content": result})
        continue

    if msg.type == "final":
        return msg.content
```

Two practical details matter a lot:
- **Guard against infinite loops** (iteration limits, timeouts, budgets).
- Prefer tools when they’re **epistemically necessary** (you can’t reliably answer from context alone).

## Planning and task decomposition

Agents shine on tasks that are naturally multi-step: “book travel,” “debug this repo,” “investigate a fraud case.”

Planning often looks like:
- breaking a goal into **subgoals**,
- ordering them,
- and revising the plan when feedback arrives (closed-loop behavior).

A useful mental model: planning is not a single moment—it’s something the agent keeps doing *between actions*.

## Memory: short-term vs long-term

Agents need memory because the world is bigger than a context window.

- **Short-term memory**: what’s in the current prompt (recent messages, current plan).
- **Long-term memory**: external storage (notes, vector DB, files) the agent can retrieve later.

Good agents treat context as a scarce resource: they **trim** and **compress** history so the model stays focused and tool calls remain accurate.

## Single-agent vs multi-agent (when to add complexity)

You can build on a spectrum:

| Level | What it is | When it’s enough |
|---|---|---|
| Direct model call | One prompt → one answer | Single-step tasks (summarize, classify, rewrite) |
| Single agent with tools | One agent loops + calls tools | Most “do work for me” workflows |
| Multi-agent orchestration | Specialized agents coordinate | Cross-domain tasks, parallel work, security boundaries |

*Table adapted from Azure agent orchestration guidance (Microsoft).* 

Multi-agent systems can be powerful, but they add coordination overhead and new failure modes—so you earn them only when a single agent can’t reliably manage the toolset or domain breadth.

## Summary

An AI agent is an LLM embedded in a loop that repeatedly observes, plans, acts through tools, and updates memory until a stop condition is reached. The core shift from “chatbot” to “agent” is **autonomous control over the next step**, not just better prompting.

## Recommended Reading
- [LLM Powered Autonomous Agents](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2023-06-23-agent/) — Clear breakdown of planning, memory, and tool use as the core pillars.
- [A practical guide to building agents](https://cdn.openai.com/business-guides-and-resources/a-practical-guide-to-building-agents.pdf) — Product/engineering view: when agents make sense and how to design them safely.
- [Function calling guide (OpenAI)](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/function-calling) — Concrete mechanics of tool calling loops.
- [The Landscape of Emerging AI Agent Architectures (survey)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.11584.pdf) — Big-picture map of single vs multi-agent patterns, planning/execution/reflection phases.
lessons.summary: AI agents are LLMs wrapped in a control loop that can plan, call tools, observe results, and update memory until a goal is achieved. What makes them “agents” is autonomous control over which step to take next, not just generating text.

image_metadata.caption: LLM agent architecture: planning, memory, and tool use components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall system structure before explaining each subsystem in detail.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: LLM-powered autonomous agent system overview. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall system structure; return to it as a reference when introducing each individual component.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: LLM-powered autonomous agent system overview. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall system structure; return to it as a reference when introducing each individual component.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, executes, and responds. (Shen et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response into a coherent pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user input flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.

image_metadata.caption: Generative agent architecture: memory, reflection, and planning modules. (Park et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching memory and planning components of LLM agents, especially to demonstrate how reflection bridges raw observations and high-level planning in a multi-agent social simulation context.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations, stores them in a memory stream, runs reflection to form higher-level insights, and uses planning to drive behavior in a simulated social environment.

image_metadata.caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, and coordinates AI models. (Shen et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response, reinforcing the four-stage pipeline described in the text.
image_metadata.description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.

image_metadata.caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, reflection, and planning enable human-like behavior. (Park et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from tool-use to agent simulation topics, or when teaching memory and planning as agent components; use it to ground the abstract idea of 'believable human behavior' in a concrete architectural design.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations through a memory stream, retrieves relevant memories via reflection, and uses a planning module to produce actions in a simulated social environment with 25 virtual characters.

image_metadata.caption: HuggingGPT: LLM orchestrates specialized models across four pipeline stages. (Shen et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, especially to demonstrate how a single LLM can coordinate many external models rather than solving everything itself.
image_metadata.description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-step prompting.
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step dialogue example showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.

image_metadata.caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths beyond linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly when comparing CoT variants and explaining how tree-search improves complex problem solving.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies: Input→Output (IO), Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts (ToT), showing how ToT explores multiple reasoning branches as a tree structure with evaluation at each node.

image_metadata.caption: PoT offloads computation to Python code, unlike CoT's natural language steps. (Chen et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs can use code as a reasoning medium, especially for arithmetic or symbolic tasks where natural language reasoning is error-prone.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.

image_metadata.caption: TALM uses special tokens to embed API calls within text generation. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing tool-augmented language models and explaining how API calls are structured within the LM's text generation pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating how the LM generates an API request, receives a result, and produces a final output within a single text sequence.

image_metadata.caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively improve tool-use performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the training methodology of TALM and the value of self-play loops for improving tool-use capabilities in LLMs.
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing model performance (accuracy or similar metric) across multiple self-play iterations in TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use examples progressively improves the model's performance.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer's pipeline: sample, execute, filter, and fine-tune on API calls. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how Toolformer differs from TALM and how self-supervised filtering of API calls enables scalable tool-use training.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram illustrating how Toolformer is built: starting from raw text, sampling candidate API call positions, executing API calls, filtering by whether results reduce language modeling loss, and fine-tuning the LM on the resulting annotated dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls for self-supervised training. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the data annotation step of Toolformer training, particularly to illustrate how multiple tool types are handled with a unified annotation format.
image_metadata.description: An annotated text example showing how Toolformer inserts API call annotations into a sentence, with calculator, Wikipedia search, and translation tool calls marked inline, demonstrating the format used to create the self-supervised training dataset.

image_metadata.caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision-making tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the ReAct framework, specifically to contrast the Thought-Action-Observation loop against an Act-only approach and explain why reasoning traces improve performance.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion's self-reflection loop: actor, evaluator, and memory buffer. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how agents can self-correct, particularly after introducing the concept of trial-and-error in autonomous agents and before discussing the heuristic function details.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle between Actor (LLM), Evaluator (reward/heuristic), Self-Reflection module, and Memory buffer, with arrows indicating how failed trajectories feed into self-reflection and updated memory for the next trial.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates over inefficient planning. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to validate its claims with data, and use the hallucination vs. inefficient planning breakdown to deepen discussion of agent failure modes.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across multiple trials.

image_metadata.caption: CoH fine-tuning: model improves outputs conditioned on ranked feedback sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how models can learn from ranked feedback sequences, especially as a contrast to RLHF, to illustrate an alternative self-reflection training approach.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, etc.) is fed as a prefix, and the model learns to produce incrementally improved outputs conditioned on that sequence.

image_metadata.caption: Algorithm Distillation: distilling RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Algorithm Distillation as a method for in-context RL, particularly to explain how learning histories are structured and used for training.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): source policies generate multi-episode learning histories for various tasks, which are then used to train a neural network via behavioral cloning to produce a task-agnostic in-context RL policy.

image_metadata.caption: AD outperforms ED and RL² on memory/exploration tasks with binary reward. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the AD mechanism to provide empirical validation, and use to discuss why multi-episode context length matters for in-context RL emergence.
image_metadata.description: Performance comparison charts of AD, ED (Expert Distillation), source policy, and RL² across memory and exploration environments (dark environments with A3C, watermaze with DQN), measuring cumulative reward over episodes with binary reward.

image_metadata.caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the memory module to establish the cognitive science analogy before explaining how each memory type is implemented in LLM agent systems.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into sensory memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), short-term/working memory, and long-term memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory equivalents.

image_metadata.caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoffs. (Google Blog, 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing external memory implementation and vector databases, specifically to explain why approximate (not exact) search is used and how to choose among ANN algorithms.
image_metadata.description: A comparison chart of Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) / Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) measured by recall@10 versus query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff.

image_metadata.caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM API-call decision logic. (Li et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how to evaluate an agent's tool use capability, particularly to contrast the three levels of API-Bank assessment and highlight where LLM reasoning can fail in a tool-augmented pipeline.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, and result evaluation across multiple decision levels.

image_metadata.caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the ReAct framework; use to contrast Act-only vs. ReAct trajectories and prompt discussion on why explicit reasoning steps matter.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion framework: actor, evaluator, and self-reflection cycle. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the concept of self-reflection; use the diagram to walk through each stage of the Reflexion loop step by step.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle of: Actor generating actions, Evaluator scoring trajectories, Self-Reflection module producing verbal feedback, and that feedback being stored in episodic memory to inform the next trial.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates AlfWorld failures. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to ground the theory in experimental results; use to discuss the relative prevalence of hallucination vs. planning failures.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across successive trials.

image_metadata.caption: CoH fine-tuning: model learns from ranked, annotated output sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when teaching CoH as a self-reflection mechanism; use to contrast with RLHF and highlight how explicit feedback sequences differ from scalar reward signals.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, Best) is fed as input, and the model learns to produce progressively improved outputs conditioned on that sequence.

image_metadata.caption: Algorithm Distillation distills RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when introducing in-context RL and AD; the diagram helps students grasp why multi-episode context is necessary and how the distillation process works.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): source RL policies generate multi-episode learning histories per task, which are concatenated and used to train a single transformer via behavioral cloning to perform in-context RL.

image_metadata.caption: AD matches RL² on memory/exploration tasks with binary reward only. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining AD to substantiate its claims; prompt students to compare AD vs. RL² and discuss what the results imply about context length requirements.
image_metadata.description: Performance comparison charts of AD, Expert Distillation (ED), source policy, and RL² on memory-and-exploration environments (dark rooms, watermaze), showing AD matches or exceeds RL² with binary rewards only.

image_metadata.caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the memory module; use the biological analogy to motivate design choices for agent memory systems before discussing technical implementations.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into Sensory Memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), Short-Term/Working Memory, and Long-Term Memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory equivalents.

image_metadata.caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoff. (Google Blog, 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing external memory retrieval mechanisms; use to motivate the choice of ANN over exact search and to compare specific algorithm tradeoffs.
image_metadata.description: A recall@10 performance comparison chart of multiple Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) / MIPS algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) plotted against query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff.

image_metadata.caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision logic for tool selection and execution. (Li et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing HuggingGPT to deepen understanding of how tool-use decisions are structured and evaluated; use it when discussing how we measure agent accuracy at each step of an API interaction.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, call execution, and result evaluation across three levels of tool-use difficulty.

image_metadata.caption: Prompt vs. context engineering: discrete prompt-writing vs iterative context curation (Anthropic).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student assumes better results come only from rewriting the prompt; use it to reframe debugging toward what information is included/excluded each agent step (retrieval results, tool traces, memory, instructions).
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side process diagram contrasting prompt engineering (a mostly one-time, discrete prompt-writing step before inference) with context engineering (an iterative loop that repeatedly curates what information—prompts plus other tokens like retrieved docs, tool outputs, memory—gets passed into the model each run).

image_metadata.caption: Calibrating system prompts: avoid brittle rules and overly general assumptions (Anthropic).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student’s agent either fails outside narrow cases (too rigid) or behaves inconsistently (too vague); use it to guide rewriting the system prompt toward explicit constraints, definitions, and boundaries without encoding exhaustive if/else logic.
image_metadata.description: A spectrum/continuum diagram for system-prompt calibration: one extreme shows brittle, hardcoded if/else-style prompts; the other shows overly general prompts that assume shared context; the middle implies a calibrated system prompt that is specific, grounded, and robust without overfitting.

image_metadata.caption: Agent workflow loop for tool use (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student asks how an agent differs from a single LLM call, or when introducing tool calling as an iterative plan-act-observe loop.
image_metadata.description: A flow diagram labeled "Agent Workflow" showing an LLM-driven agent loop: user input goes to an agent/LLM, the agent selects and calls tools, observes tool results, updates state/memory, and iterates until producing a final answer.

image_metadata.caption: Common ways to augment an LLM: tools, retrieval, memory (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when deciding between RAG, tools, or memory for a requirement (fresh data, actions, personalization).
image_metadata.description: A diagram titled "LLM augmentations" illustrating an LLM connected to external capabilities such as tools/functions, retrieval over a knowledge base (RAG), memory/state, and possibly structured outputs—showing how inputs/contexts are augmented before generating responses.

image_metadata.caption: Prompt chaining: sequential LLM steps with intermediate outputs (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student struggles with one-shot prompts and needs a step-by-step pipeline (e.g., extract then transform then summarize).
image_metadata.description: A "Prompt chaining" diagram showing a linear pipeline where the output of one LLM prompt feeds into the next step, forming a multi-step sequence from input to final output.

image_metadata.caption: Parallelization: fan out tasks, then aggregate results (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when optimizing response time or when tasks are independent (e.g., gather facts from multiple sources simultaneously).
image_metadata.description: A "Parallelization" diagram showing one input branching into multiple LLM/tool tasks executed concurrently, then merging results into a combined final response.

image_metadata.caption: Routing: choose a specialized path based on the request (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when building multi-skill assistants and explaining how to choose between specialized handlers (e.g., math vs. search vs. database).
image_metadata.description: A "Routing" diagram where an initial classifier/decision step sends the request to one of several specialized branches (different prompts, tools, or agents), each producing an output that becomes the final answer.

image_metadata.caption: Orchestrator delegates subtasks to workers and aggregates outputs (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student needs to scale beyond a single agent—e.g., splitting research, coding, and verification into separate workers.
image_metadata.description: An "Orchestrator-worker" diagram showing a central orchestrator that decomposes a task and dispatches subtasks to multiple worker components/agents, then collects their outputs to produce a final result.

image_metadata.caption: Evaluator-optimizer loop for iterative quality improvement (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to improve outputs with critique steps (e.g., rewrite until it meets rubric/constraints).
image_metadata.description: An "Evaluator-optimizer" diagram showing an iterative loop where an optimizer (generator) produces an output, an evaluator critiques/scores it, and feedback is used to refine the next attempt until acceptable quality is reached.

image_metadata.caption: Agent architecture: LLM with tools and state/memory (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of an agents lesson or when clarifying the components required to build a tool-using assistant.
image_metadata.description: An "Agents" diagram depicting an agent centered on an LLM connected to a set of tools and a state/memory component, showing the agent deciding which tool to call and using results to answer the user.

image_metadata.caption: Agent workflow: decide, call tools, observe, iterate (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing what an agent is, or when a student is confused about how tool results feed back into the model before the final answer.
image_metadata.description: A high-level agent workflow diagram showing a user request going to an LLM-based agent, the agent deciding to call tools, executing tool calls, observing tool results, and iterating until producing a final response.

image_metadata.caption: Common LLM augmentations: tools, retrieval, and memory (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing approaches (pure prompting vs tools vs RAG) or when deciding which augmentation to use for a task.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of an LLM augmented by external capabilities, typically including tools/functions, retrieval or knowledge sources, and memory/context, connected around the core model to improve responses.

image_metadata.caption: Prompt chaining: sequential LLM steps with intermediate outputs (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student’s single prompt is failing and you want to teach splitting the task into sequential stages with checkpoints.
image_metadata.description: A prompt-chaining diagram showing multiple sequential LLM steps where the output of one prompt becomes the input to the next, forming a linear pipeline to reach a final result.

image_metadata.caption: Parallelization: fan-out work, then aggregate results (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when optimizing for speed or breadth (e.g., generating multiple candidates, evaluating options, or researching multiple facets simultaneously).
image_metadata.description: A parallelization diagram where a task is split into multiple branches processed concurrently (often by multiple LLM calls or workers) and then aggregated into a combined result.

image_metadata.caption: Routing: send requests to specialized paths based on intent (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to handle heterogeneous queries (e.g., math vs policy vs retrieval) by selecting specialized handlers.
image_metadata.description: A routing diagram showing an input being classified or inspected and then directed to one of several specialized paths/models/tools, with the selected route producing the output.

image_metadata.caption: Worker pattern: coordinator assigns tasks to workers and collects outputs (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how to scale an agent workflow to many subtasks or how to structure a graph with coordinator and worker nodes.
image_metadata.description: A worker pattern diagram showing a coordinator distributing tasks to one or more workers (often LLM calls or tool-executing nodes) and collecting their outputs.

image_metadata.caption: Evaluator-optimizer: evaluate outputs, then revise iteratively (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student needs higher reliability (e.g., formatting constraints, correctness checks) and you want to introduce evaluation + revision cycles.
image_metadata.description: An evaluator-optimizer loop diagram where an output is evaluated (by rules or an LLM judge), feedback is produced, and an optimizer revises the solution iteratively until it meets criteria.

image_metadata.caption: Agent with tools: select actions, observe results, answer (LangChain).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first defining an agent versus a simple chain, or when explaining how tool availability changes what the model can do.
image_metadata.description: An agent diagram showing an LLM agent connected to a set of tools, with a loop indicating repeated tool selection and execution before returning a final answer to the user.

image_metadata.caption: SQL agent as a subgraph node in a parent LangGraph (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how to add a subgraph as a node, or when a learner is confused about how nested graphs interact with parent state (different vs shared schemas).
image_metadata.description: A LangGraph diagram of an SQL agent workflow, showing a parent graph node that encapsulates a subgraph (the SQL agent) with internal steps for interpreting a question, generating SQL, executing it, and returning an answer.

image_metadata.caption: SQL agent subgraph architecture overview (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show during recap or Q&A (e.g., 'Was this page helpful?') to anchor discussion back to the concrete subgraph architecture.
image_metadata.description: The same LangGraph SQL agent diagram as Image 3, depicting a subgraph-based SQL agent workflow embedded as a node within a larger graph.

image_metadata.caption: Sequential agent pipeline: output of each stage feeds the next (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner is deciding between single-agent vs multi-agent designs, or when explaining how to decompose a task into ordered stages with clear handoffs.
image_metadata.description: A linear pipeline diagram: Input flows to Agent 1, then Agent 2, then an ellipsis indicating more stages, then Agent n, ending at Result. Each agent is connected to its own supporting box labeled "Model, knowledge, and tools," emphasizing that every stage has dedicated capabilities.

image_metadata.caption: Contract drafting via sequential specialist agents (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to operationalize sequential orchestration in business processes (e.g., contract drafting) and how to assign tools/knowledge per stage.
image_metadata.description: A sequential document-generation pipeline for a law firm: Document creation requirements flow to a Template selection agent (supported by a model, template library, and research tools), then to a Clause customization agent (supported by a fine-tuned model and related resources), continuing through additional specialized agents to produce a final contract/document result.

image_metadata.caption: Concurrent agents with collector aggregation (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to get multiple independent analyses quickly, or when discussing how to design an aggregator/collector to reconcile conflicting outputs.
image_metadata.description: A fan-out/fan-in architecture: Input goes to an Initiator and collector agent, which dispatches the same task to multiple parallel agents (Agent 1, Agent 2, …, Agent n). Each produces intermediate results that return to the collector, which outputs aggregated results based on combining, comparing, and selecting among agent outputs.

image_metadata.caption: Parallel specialist agents evaluate a stock, then synthesize evidence (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching concurrent orchestration in decision-support settings, especially where multiple analytic lenses must be synthesized into one justified recommendation.
image_metadata.description: A concurrent stock-evaluation example: A ticker symbol goes to a Stock analysis agent (with model and exchange-symbol-mapping knowledge). The agent fans out to multiple specialist analysis agents (e.g., different analysis types), gathers their intermediate results, and produces a decision with supporting evidence based on combined intermediate outputs.

image_metadata.caption: Chat manager coordinates multi-agent discussion with shared thread (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining collaborative multi-agent workflows, especially when learners need to understand how a manager controls participation and uses the evolving thread as working memory.
image_metadata.description: A managed multi-agent conversation: Input goes to a Group chat manager. The manager uses a model to coordinate which agents respond next and maintains an Accumulating chat thread. The diagram indicates the manager can issue new group instructions based on accumulated context, guiding the conversation toward outcomes.

image_metadata.caption: Specialist agents debate park proposals with checks and iteration limits (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner is implementing group chat patterns and needs concrete guidance on stopping conditions, quality checks, and escalation paths.
image_metadata.description: A group-chat example for municipal park planning: the system ingests a draft park proposal and routes it into a managed debate among specialist city-planning agents (different community-impact perspectives). A checker/acceptance step and iteration cap are implied to prevent endless refinement, with fallback to human review or best-effort output with warnings.

image_metadata.caption: Dynamic agent handoffs route work to specialists or humans (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how to route requests to the right expert agent, or when designing escalation paths and ownership transfer in support workflows.
image_metadata.description: A dynamic handoff diagram: Agent 1 receives input and uses a model with general knowledge to produce a result or route the task via curved handoff arrows to other specialist agents (Agent 2, Agent 3, …, Agent n), each with their own model/knowledge/tools and result. A customer support employee appears as a possible endpoint for escalation/handling.

image_metadata.caption: CRM triage agent hands off to telecom specialists for one final answer (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when learners are building routed support assistants and need to understand single-agent ownership, handoff criteria, and safeguards against repeated transfers.
image_metadata.description: A telecom CRM support example of handoff: a Triage support agent (model + general knowledge) takes customer input and either resolves it or hands off to the most appropriate specialist (e.g., Technical infrastructure agent with infrastructure knowledge/tools, plus other domain agents). Only one agent operates at a time, producing a single final result after the handoff chain.

image_metadata.caption: Magentic manager builds and tracks a dynamic task ledger (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner faces an ill-defined problem requiring iterative planning and execution, and needs a structure for creating, refining, and tracking tasks over time.
image_metadata.description: A magentic orchestration overview: a magentic manager agent collaborates with specialized agents to dynamically build and refine a task ledger (goals/subgoals). The workflow emphasizes documenting the approach, then finalizing and tracking tasks to completion, often with agents that can take actions in external systems.

image_metadata.caption: Magentic orchestration for SRE incident remediation planning and execution (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to automate open-ended operational workflows (incident triage/remediation) and how to coordinate diagnostics, rollback, and communications under a single planning manager.
image_metadata.description: An SRE automation magentic example: an SRE automation manager agent (input + model) writes to a Task and progress ledger, then invokes multiple knowledge/action agents (Infrastructure, Diagnostics, Rollback, Communication). The manager evaluates goals/progress and iterates, aiming to remediate low-risk incidents without predefined steps.

image_metadata.caption: CrewAI Crew Studio Task Builder interface (CrewAI Docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to create a task in Crew Studio or what fields are required to define a task; use it to walk through each field and have the learner draft a task description and expected output that matches the UI inputs.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of the CrewAI Crew Studio Task Builder interface showing a form to define a task (task name/description, expected output, assigned agent, tools, context/inputs, and configuration options) within a visual workflow UI.

image_metadata.caption: CrewAI Flows: orchestration backbone for multi-agent tasks (CrewAI Docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CrewAI at a high level or when a learner confuses agent roles with orchestration; use it to map a real task into ordered steps and identify where handoffs occur.
image_metadata.description: A framework overview diagram highlighting 'Flows' as the backbone of CrewAI, showing an orchestrated sequence/pipeline that coordinates steps and handoffs to enable multiple AI agents to work together on a complex task.

image_metadata.caption: CrewAI Crews: teams of role-based agents providing intelligence (CrewAI Docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to design an agent team: selecting roles, assigning responsibilities, and deciding which agent produces which intermediate artifact before integrating results.
image_metadata.description: A framework overview diagram emphasizing 'Crews' as the intelligence layer in CrewAI, depicting a team of agents with defined roles collaborating to complete tasks within an orchestrated process.

image_metadata.caption: Run lifecycle status transitions (OpenAI Assistants API docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student is implementing run polling/streaming logic, debugging why a run is stuck, or deciding what to do on each status (e.g., handle requires_action vs stop on completed/failed/cancelled/expired).
image_metadata.description: A state-transition diagram for an Assistants API Run object, showing how a run moves through statuses such as queued, in_progress, requires_action (tool calls), completed, failed, cancelled, and expired, including possible transitions between active and terminal states.

image_metadata.caption: Run step lifecycle status transitions (OpenAI Assistants API docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student needs to interpret run_steps output, build progress indicators, or debug tool execution by examining step_details and step statuses while a run is in_progress.
concepts: Tool call step vs message creation step
image_metadata.description: A state-transition diagram for Run Steps, illustrating that each step progresses through the same kinds of statuses as a Run (e.g., queued, in_progress, requires_action, completed, failed, cancelled, expired) and ends in a terminal state, representing the lifecycle of individual actions within a run.

image_metadata.caption: LangGraph in-memory checkpointing with InMemorySaver (AWS Database Blog).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing LangGraph persistence/checkpointing, or when a student asks why state is lost after restarting a prototype service.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of LangGraph checkpointing using InMemorySaver: a workflow executes a super-step through graph nodes from START to END while a checkpointer continuously writes state snapshots and execution history to in-memory storage, enabling persistence-like features during a single process lifetime.

image_metadata.caption: Durable LangGraph checkpoints using DynamoDBSaver and Amazon DynamoDB (AWS Database Blog).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from prototyping to production, discussing long-running conversations, or explaining how to persist agent state across restarts using DynamoDB.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of LangGraph checkpointing using DynamoDBSaver: the same START-to-END graph execution writes checkpoints to Amazon DynamoDB instead of process memory, providing durable, scalable storage for conversation state and execution history across long-running sessions and restarts.

image_metadata.caption: Checkpointing saves state snapshots for resuming runs (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing persistence/checkpointing or when a student asks how LangGraph can resume after interruptions.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating LangGraph persistence via checkpoints: a graph/agent run progresses through steps while periodically saving state snapshots (checkpoints) to a persistent store so execution can be resumed later.

image_metadata.caption: Get state history by querying saved checkpoints (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to inspect past execution states or when debugging unexpected agent behavior across steps.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing retrieval of state history for a LangGraph run: multiple saved checkpoints are listed/queried to view prior states over time.

image_metadata.caption: Replay resumes execution from a chosen checkpoint (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student wants to reproduce a bug, compare outcomes, or continue a run from a known-good intermediate state.
image_metadata.description: Diagram depicting replay: re-running a LangGraph execution from a selected checkpoint/state to reproduce behavior or continue execution from that point.

image_metadata.caption: Update persisted state to continue or branch a run (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how to intervene in a run (edit state) or when demonstrating branching/what-if analysis from persisted checkpoints.
image_metadata.description: Multi-step diagram showing updating state: an existing persisted checkpoint/state is modified (patched) and then used to continue or branch execution, illustrating a full story from initial run to updated continuation.

image_metadata.caption: Checkpointing saves state snapshots for resuming runs (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing checkpointing in the Chat LangChain section or when learners need a quick refresher on persistence.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the checkpointing diagram: a run progresses through steps while saving periodic state checkpoints to persistent storage for later resumption.

image_metadata.caption: Get state history by querying saved checkpoints (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching state inspection in the Chat LangChain section or when diagnosing where state diverged.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the state history diagram: querying multiple checkpoints to view the sequence of prior states for a run/thread.

image_metadata.caption: Replay resumes execution from a chosen checkpoint (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining replay in the Chat LangChain section or when a learner asks how to reproduce a prior run.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the replay diagram: selecting a checkpoint and replaying execution from that saved state.

image_metadata.caption: Update persisted state to continue or branch a run (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when demonstrating state edits/branching in the Chat LangChain section or when teaching human-in-the-loop workflows.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the update-state full story diagram: modifying a persisted checkpoint/state and continuing execution, potentially creating a new branch of the run.

image_metadata.caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the ReAct framework; use to contrast Act-only vs. ReAct trajectories and prompt discussion on why explicit reasoning steps matter.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion framework: actor, evaluator, and self-reflection cycle. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the concept of self-reflection; use the diagram to walk through each stage of the Reflexion loop step by step.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle of: Actor generating actions, Evaluator scoring trajectories, Self-Reflection module producing verbal feedback, and that feedback being stored in episodic memory to inform the next trial.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates AlfWorld failures. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to ground the theory in experimental results; use to discuss the relative prevalence of hallucination vs. planning failures.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across successive trials.

image_metadata.caption: CoH fine-tuning: model learns from ranked, annotated output sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when teaching CoH as a self-reflection mechanism; use to contrast with RLHF and highlight how explicit feedback sequences differ from scalar reward signals.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, Best) is fed as input, and the model learns to produce progressively improved outputs conditioned on that sequence.

image_metadata.caption: Algorithm Distillation distills RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when introducing in-context RL and AD; the diagram helps students grasp why multi-episode context is necessary and how the distillation process works.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): source RL policies generate multi-episode learning histories per task, which are concatenated and used to train a single transformer via behavioral cloning to perform in-context RL.

image_metadata.caption: AD matches RL² on memory/exploration tasks with binary reward only. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining AD to substantiate its claims; prompt students to compare AD vs. RL² and discuss what the results imply about context length requirements.
image_metadata.description: Performance comparison charts of AD, Expert Distillation (ED), source policy, and RL² on memory-and-exploration environments (dark rooms, watermaze), showing AD matches or exceeds RL² with binary rewards only.

image_metadata.caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the memory module; use the biological analogy to motivate design choices for agent memory systems before discussing technical implementations.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into Sensory Memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), Short-Term/Working Memory, and Long-Term Memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory equivalents.

image_metadata.caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoff. (Google Blog, 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing external memory retrieval mechanisms; use to motivate the choice of ANN over exact search and to compare specific algorithm tradeoffs.
image_metadata.description: A recall@10 performance comparison chart of multiple Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) / MIPS algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) plotted against query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff.

image_metadata.caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision logic for tool API calls. (Li et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how to evaluate or benchmark an agent's tool use ability, or when explaining the granular decisions an LLM must make correctly to successfully call an external API.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark: checking API availability, forming the call, executing it, and evaluating the result at multiple decision points.

image_metadata.caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, reflection, and planning modules. (Park et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching memory and planning components of LLM agents, or when discussing how agents maintain coherent identity and behavior over extended interactions in a simulation.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a generative agent showing three interconnected components: a memory stream (log of experiences), a reflection mechanism (synthesizing higher-level insights), and a planning module, all feeding into the agent's actions within a simulated environment.

image_metadata.caption: Function-calling agent flow: interpret, call tool, use results, respond (DAIR.AI).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing why LLMs need tools for real-time/authoritative data, or when walking through a concrete tool-calling example (weather lookup) to map each step from user query to tool call to final response.
image_metadata.description: A flow diagram showing an AI agent loop: a user asks a question (e.g., weather in Paris), the LLM decides it needs external data, generates a structured function/tool call with parameters (city=Paris), sends it to an external API/tool, receives the tool result, and then the LLM composes a final natural-language answer back to the user.

lessons.title: The ReAct Pattern: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting
lessons.content:
# The ReAct Pattern: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting

If you’ve ever watched an LLM confidently invent a “fact” it can’t possibly know, you’ve seen the core failure mode of **reasoning without grounding**. The model is great at producing plausible text, and sometimes even great at multi-step logic—but if a step requires *fresh information* (or a real-world action), it may just… guess.

**ReAct** is a simple but powerful fix: make the model alternate between **thinking** and **doing**, and force it to incorporate what it *observes* from the world.

![ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision-making tasks. (Adapted from Lilian Weng)](/api/wiki-images/agent-fundamentals/images/lilianweng-posts-2023-06-23-agent_002.png)

## The core loop: Thought → Action → Observation

ReAct is an agent pattern where the LLM produces an interleaved trace like:

- **Thought**: internal reasoning / plan update
- **Action**: a tool call (search, database query, calculator, environment step)
- **Observation**: the tool’s result, fed back to the model

Then repeat until the model can answer.

Why this helps:

1. **Reasoning traces** keep the model organized: it can decompose the task, track progress, and notice when it’s stuck.
2. **Actions** let the model stop relying on its memory of the world and instead *check*.
3. **Observations** create a paper trail: you can debug where things went wrong (bad query? wrong tool? wrong interpretation?).

## ReAct vs. “reason-only” and “act-only”

A useful mental model is: reasoning is for *deciding what to do next*; acting is for *getting reliable evidence*.

| Approach | What it’s good at | Common failure mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Reason-only (CoT)** | Decomposing and synthesizing | Hallucinating missing facts; error snowballing |
| **Act-only** | Fetching lots of info | Failing to connect the dots into a final answer |
| **ReAct** | Both: plan + evidence + synthesis | Still can loop or misread observations, but is debuggable |

*Table note: grounded tool observations reduce hallucinations by replacing “guessing” with “checking.”*

## A tiny ReAct-style example (tool calling)

Imagine a question: “What’s the weather in Paris right now?” The model shouldn’t answer from memory—it should call a weather tool.

Here’s the *shape* of a ReAct loop using tool calling (pseudo-Python):

```python
messages = [
  {"role": "user", "content": "What's the weather in Paris right now?"}
]

while True:
    resp = model(messages, tools=[get_weather])

    if resp.tool_call is None:
        print(resp.final_text)
        break

    # Action
    tool_name, args = resp.tool_call.name, resp.tool_call.args
    observation = run_tool(tool_name, args)

    # Observation
    messages.append(resp.as_message())
    messages.append({
        "role": "tool",
        "name": tool_name,
        "content": observation
    })
```

Notice what’s happening architecturally: your application is the “hands.” The model proposes an action; your code executes it; the result comes back as an observation.

## Designing good ReAct prompts (practical tips)

- **Make actions explicit and constrained.** Tools should have clear names, descriptions, and argument schemas.
- **Teach the loop with a few trajectories.** A couple of high-quality examples showing Thought/Action/Observation often beats lots of vague instruction.
- **Let the model decide when to think vs. act.** In interactive tasks, sparse thoughts can be enough—overthinking can waste tokens.
- **Log everything.** ReAct shines when you can inspect the trace and say: “Ah, the search query was wrong,” instead of “the model is bad.”

## Summary

ReAct turns an LLM from a single-shot text generator into a grounded problem solver by interleaving reasoning with tool use. The Thought–Action–Observation loop reduces hallucinations, improves multi-step reliability, and—crucially—makes failures inspectable and fixable.

## Recommended Reading

- [ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models](https://react-lm.github.io) — The original ReAct idea and examples across QA and interactive environments.
- [Function calling (tool calling) guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/function-calling) — How to implement the action/observation loop with structured tool calls.
- [LLM Powered Autonomous Agents](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2023-06-23-agent/) — A broader agent framework: planning, memory, tool use, and reflection.
- [LangGraph agentic concepts](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/agentic_concepts/) — How to model agent loops as stateful graphs for debugging and production.
lessons.summary: ReAct is an agent pattern where an LLM alternates between reasoning and tool-based actions, using observations to update its plan. This grounding via a Thought–Action–Observation loop reduces hallucinations and makes multi-step behavior easier to debug.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-step prompting.
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step dialogue example showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.

image_metadata.caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision-making tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the ReAct framework, specifically to contrast the Thought-Action-Observation loop against an Act-only approach and explain why reasoning traces improve performance.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.

image_metadata.caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, executes, and responds. (Shen et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response into a coherent pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user input flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-pass prompting.
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step dialogue showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.

image_metadata.caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the ReAct framework; use to contrast Act-only vs. ReAct trajectories and prompt discussion on why explicit reasoning steps matter.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.

image_metadata.caption: HuggingGPT: LLM orchestrates specialized models across four pipeline stages. (Shen et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, especially to demonstrate how a single LLM can coordinate many external models rather than solving everything itself.
image_metadata.description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.

image_metadata.caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths beyond linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly when comparing CoT variants and explaining how tree-search improves complex problem solving.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies: Input→Output (IO), Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts (ToT), showing how ToT explores multiple reasoning branches as a tree structure with evaluation at each node.

image_metadata.caption: PoT offloads computation to Python code, unlike CoT's natural language steps. (Chen et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs can use code as a reasoning medium, especially for arithmetic or symbolic tasks where natural language reasoning is error-prone.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.

image_metadata.caption: TALM uses special tokens to embed API calls within text generation. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing tool-augmented language models and explaining how API calls are structured within the LM's text generation pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating how the LM generates an API request, receives a result, and produces a final output within a single text sequence.

image_metadata.caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively improve tool-use performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the training methodology of TALM and the value of self-play loops for improving tool-use capabilities in LLMs.
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing model performance (accuracy or similar metric) across multiple self-play iterations in TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use examples progressively improves the model's performance.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer's pipeline: sample, execute, filter, and fine-tune on API calls. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how Toolformer differs from TALM and how self-supervised filtering of API calls enables scalable tool-use training.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram illustrating how Toolformer is built: starting from raw text, sampling candidate API call positions, executing API calls, filtering by whether results reduce language modeling loss, and fine-tuning the LM on the resulting annotated dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls for self-supervised training. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the data annotation step of Toolformer training, particularly to illustrate how multiple tool types are handled with a unified annotation format.
image_metadata.description: An annotated text example showing how Toolformer inserts API call annotations into a sentence, with calculator, Wikipedia search, and translation tool calls marked inline, demonstrating the format used to create the self-supervised training dataset.

image_metadata.caption: LLM agent architecture: planning, memory, and tool use components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall system structure before explaining each subsystem in detail.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion's self-reflection loop: actor, evaluator, and memory buffer. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how agents can self-correct, particularly after introducing the concept of trial-and-error in autonomous agents and before discussing the heuristic function details.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle between Actor (LLM), Evaluator (reward/heuristic), Self-Reflection module, and Memory buffer, with arrows indicating how failed trajectories feed into self-reflection and updated memory for the next trial.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates over inefficient planning. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to validate its claims with data, and use the hallucination vs. inefficient planning breakdown to deepen discussion of agent failure modes.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across multiple trials.

image_metadata.caption: CoH fine-tuning: model improves outputs conditioned on ranked feedback sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how models can learn from ranked feedback sequences, especially as a contrast to RLHF, to illustrate an alternative self-reflection training approach.

image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): source policies generate multi-episode learning histories for various tasks, which are then used to train a neural network via behavioral cloning to produce a task-agnostic in-context RL policy.

image_metadata.description: A comparison chart of Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) / Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) measured by recall@10 versus query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff.

image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, and result evaluation across multiple decision levels.

image_metadata.caption: Generative agent architecture: memory, reflection, and planning modules. (Park et al. 2023)

image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations, stores them in a memory stream, runs reflection to form higher-level insights, and uses planning to drive behavior in a simulated social environment.

image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies side by side: Input→Output (standard), Chain-of-Thought, Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts — showing how ToT explores a branching tree of intermediate reasoning steps with evaluation and backtracking.

image_metadata.caption: PoT generates executable code instead of verbal reasoning steps. (Chen et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing programming-language-based prompting methods (PAL/PoT), especially to contrast with CoT and motivate why symbolic execution is more reliable for math.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.

image_metadata.caption: TALM uses special tokens to structure tool calls and results. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs interface with external tools at the token level, before or alongside Toolformer to compare approaches.
image_metadata.description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating where the LLM generates a tool request, where the external API result is inserted, and where the final answer is produced.

image_metadata.caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively improve model performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the TALM self-play mechanism to provide quantitative justification for why iterative tool-use training works.
image_metadata.description: A performance graph showing model accuracy across multiple self-play iterations in TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use training examples progressively improves the model's performance on downstream tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer pipeline: annotate, execute, filter, and fine-tune on API calls. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching Toolformer as a case study in self-supervised tool augmentation, after introducing TALM to highlight the shared self-play philosophy and differences in implementation.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram of Toolformer's training process: starting from raw text, generating candidate API call annotations via in-context learning, executing the API calls, filtering annotations by whether they reduce loss on future tokens, and fine-tuning the LM on the filtered dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls for calculator, search, and calendar. (Schick et al. 2023)

image_metadata.description: A concrete text example showing how Toolformer annotates a sentence with API calls — embedding calculator, Wikipedia search, and calendar tool calls inline within natural text, with results inserted, demonstrating the annotation format used to create training data.

image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion framework: actor, evaluator, and self-reflection cycle. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the concept of self-reflection; use the diagram to walk through each stage of the Reflexion loop step by step.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle of: Actor generating actions, Evaluator scoring trajectories, Self-Reflection module producing verbal feedback, and that feedback being stored in episodic memory to inform the next trial.

image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across successive trials.
