image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching memory and planning components of LLM agents, especially to demonstrate how reflection bridges raw observations and high-level planning in a multi-agent social simulation context.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations, stores them in a memory stream, runs reflection to form higher-level insights, and uses planning to drive behavior in a simulated social environment.

image_metadata.caption: LLM-powered autonomous agent system overview with planning, memory, and tools. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall framework; return to it as a reference when introducing each individual component.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge and decision tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the ReAct framework; use the side-by-side format to contrast reasoning-augmented vs. Act-only approaches and discuss why Thought steps improve performance.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion framework: actor, evaluator, and self-reflection feedback loop. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Reflexion after ReAct; use the cycle diagram to explain how the memory buffer accumulates reflections and how this differs from standard RL reward signals.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle between Actor (LLM), Evaluator, Self-Reflection model, and Memory buffer, with trajectories feeding back as verbal reinforcement signals for iterative improvement.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion results on AlfWorld and HotpotQA; hallucination dominates failures. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to ground the theory in experimental results; prompt students to analyze why hallucination dominates over inefficient planning as a failure mode.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across multiple trial iterations.

image_metadata.caption: Chain of Hindsight fine-tuning produces incrementally improved outputs from feedback. (Liu et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when covering self-reflection mechanisms beyond prompting; contrast with Reflexion to show a training-time vs. inference-time approach to agent self-improvement.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning process, where a model receives a sequence of past outputs annotated with feedback ranked by quality, and learns to produce incrementally better outputs conditioned on that history.

image_metadata.caption: Algorithm Distillation distills RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing advanced self-improvement mechanisms; contrast with CoH and Reflexion to show a third paradigm where the improvement process itself is learned from historical trajectories.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD), showing how multi-episode learning histories from source RL policies are used to train a neural network via behavioral cloning, enabling in-context reinforcement learning.

image_metadata.caption: AD vs. ED, source policy, and RL² on memory/exploration tasks with binary reward. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining AD to validate the approach empirically; use to discuss the role of context window length in enabling in-context RL and the trade-offs between different distillation methods.
image_metadata.description: Performance comparison charts of AD, Expert Distillation (ED), source policy, and RL² across memory and exploration environments (dark environments with A3C, watermaze with DQN), measuring learning efficiency with binary rewards.

image_metadata.caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the memory component section; use the human analogy to motivate why agent memory is structured the way it is before introducing technical implementations like vector stores.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical categorization diagram of human memory types: sensory memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), short-term/working memory, and long-term memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), mapped to their LLM agent analogues.

image_metadata.caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed trade-off. (Google Blog, 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when discussing external memory implementation; use to explain why approximate methods are preferred over exact search and how to evaluate the accuracy-latency trade-off in production systems.
image_metadata.description: A recall@10 performance comparison chart of multiple approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) / MIPS algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) plotted against query speed, showing the accuracy-speed trade-off.

image_metadata.caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, and coordinates AI models. (Shen et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response, reinforcing the four-stage pipeline described in the text.
image_metadata.description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.

image_metadata.caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision logic for API selection and execution. (Li et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Surface this after explaining HuggingGPT to deepen understanding of how tool-use accuracy is measured; use it when discussing how benchmarks decompose agent capability into discrete, testable decision points.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, and call execution with evaluation checkpoints at each step.

image_metadata.caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, reflection, and planning enable simulated human behavior. (Park et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from tool-use to memory and planning components; use it to anchor discussion of how persistent memory and self-reflection enable complex, human-like agent behavior over extended time horizons.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations through a memory stream, retrieves relevant memories via reflection, and uses a planning module to produce actions in a simulated social environment with 25 virtual characters.

image_metadata.caption: SQL agent as a subgraph node in a parent LangGraph (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how to add a subgraph as a node, or when a learner is confused about how nested graphs interact with parent state (different vs shared schemas).
image_metadata.description: A LangGraph diagram of an SQL agent workflow, showing a parent graph node that encapsulates a subgraph (the SQL agent) with internal steps for interpreting a question, generating SQL, executing it, and returning an answer.

image_metadata.caption: SQL agent subgraph architecture overview (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show during recap or Q&A (e.g., 'Was this page helpful?') to anchor discussion back to the concrete subgraph architecture.
image_metadata.description: The same LangGraph SQL agent diagram as Image 3, depicting a subgraph-based SQL agent workflow embedded as a node within a larger graph.

image_metadata.caption: Sequential agent pipeline: output of each stage feeds the next (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner is deciding between single-agent vs multi-agent designs, or when explaining how to decompose a task into ordered stages with clear handoffs.
image_metadata.description: A linear pipeline diagram: Input flows to Agent 1, then Agent 2, then an ellipsis indicating more stages, then Agent n, ending at Result. Each agent is connected to its own supporting box labeled "Model, knowledge, and tools," emphasizing that every stage has dedicated capabilities.

image_metadata.caption: Contract drafting via sequential specialist agents (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to operationalize sequential orchestration in business processes (e.g., contract drafting) and how to assign tools/knowledge per stage.
image_metadata.description: A sequential document-generation pipeline for a law firm: Document creation requirements flow to a Template selection agent (supported by a model, template library, and research tools), then to a Clause customization agent (supported by a fine-tuned model and related resources), continuing through additional specialized agents to produce a final contract/document result.

image_metadata.caption: Concurrent agents with collector aggregation (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to get multiple independent analyses quickly, or when discussing how to design an aggregator/collector to reconcile conflicting outputs.
image_metadata.description: A fan-out/fan-in architecture: Input goes to an Initiator and collector agent, which dispatches the same task to multiple parallel agents (Agent 1, Agent 2, …, Agent n). Each produces intermediate results that return to the collector, which outputs aggregated results based on combining, comparing, and selecting among agent outputs.

image_metadata.caption: Parallel specialist agents evaluate a stock, then synthesize evidence (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching concurrent orchestration in decision-support settings, especially where multiple analytic lenses must be synthesized into one justified recommendation.
image_metadata.description: A concurrent stock-evaluation example: A ticker symbol goes to a Stock analysis agent (with model and exchange-symbol-mapping knowledge). The agent fans out to multiple specialist analysis agents (e.g., different analysis types), gathers their intermediate results, and produces a decision with supporting evidence based on combined intermediate outputs.

image_metadata.caption: Chat manager coordinates multi-agent discussion with shared thread (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining collaborative multi-agent workflows, especially when learners need to understand how a manager controls participation and uses the evolving thread as working memory.
image_metadata.description: A managed multi-agent conversation: Input goes to a Group chat manager. The manager uses a model to coordinate which agents respond next and maintains an Accumulating chat thread. The diagram indicates the manager can issue new group instructions based on accumulated context, guiding the conversation toward outcomes.

image_metadata.caption: Specialist agents debate park proposals with checks and iteration limits (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner is implementing group chat patterns and needs concrete guidance on stopping conditions, quality checks, and escalation paths.
image_metadata.description: A group-chat example for municipal park planning: the system ingests a draft park proposal and routes it into a managed debate among specialist city-planning agents (different community-impact perspectives). A checker/acceptance step and iteration cap are implied to prevent endless refinement, with fallback to human review or best-effort output with warnings.

image_metadata.caption: Dynamic agent handoffs route work to specialists or humans (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how to route requests to the right expert agent, or when designing escalation paths and ownership transfer in support workflows.
image_metadata.description: A dynamic handoff diagram: Agent 1 receives input and uses a model with general knowledge to produce a result or route the task via curved handoff arrows to other specialist agents (Agent 2, Agent 3, …, Agent n), each with their own model/knowledge/tools and result. A customer support employee appears as a possible endpoint for escalation/handling.

image_metadata.caption: CRM triage agent hands off to telecom specialists for one final answer (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when learners are building routed support assistants and need to understand single-agent ownership, handoff criteria, and safeguards against repeated transfers.
image_metadata.description: A telecom CRM support example of handoff: a Triage support agent (model + general knowledge) takes customer input and either resolves it or hands off to the most appropriate specialist (e.g., Technical infrastructure agent with infrastructure knowledge/tools, plus other domain agents). Only one agent operates at a time, producing a single final result after the handoff chain.

image_metadata.caption: Magentic manager builds and tracks a dynamic task ledger (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner faces an ill-defined problem requiring iterative planning and execution, and needs a structure for creating, refining, and tracking tasks over time.
image_metadata.description: A magentic orchestration overview: a magentic manager agent collaborates with specialized agents to dynamically build and refine a task ledger (goals/subgoals). The workflow emphasizes documenting the approach, then finalizing and tracking tasks to completion, often with agents that can take actions in external systems.

image_metadata.caption: Magentic orchestration for SRE incident remediation planning and execution (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to automate open-ended operational workflows (incident triage/remediation) and how to coordinate diagnostics, rollback, and communications under a single planning manager.
image_metadata.description: An SRE automation magentic example: an SRE automation manager agent (input + model) writes to a Task and progress ledger, then invokes multiple knowledge/action agents (Infrastructure, Diagnostics, Rollback, Communication). The manager evaluates goals/progress and iterates, aiming to remediate low-risk incidents without predefined steps.

image_metadata.caption: CrewAI Crew Studio Task Builder interface (CrewAI Docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to create a task in Crew Studio or what fields are required to define a task; use it to walk through each field and have the learner draft a task description and expected output that matches the UI inputs.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of the CrewAI Crew Studio Task Builder interface showing a form to define a task (task name/description, expected output, assigned agent, tools, context/inputs, and configuration options) within a visual workflow UI.

image_metadata.caption: CrewAI Flows: orchestration backbone for multi-agent tasks (CrewAI Docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CrewAI at a high level or when a learner confuses agent roles with orchestration; use it to map a real task into ordered steps and identify where handoffs occur.
image_metadata.description: A framework overview diagram highlighting 'Flows' as the backbone of CrewAI, showing an orchestrated sequence/pipeline that coordinates steps and handoffs to enable multiple AI agents to work together on a complex task.

image_metadata.caption: CrewAI Crews: teams of role-based agents providing intelligence (CrewAI Docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to design an agent team: selecting roles, assigning responsibilities, and deciding which agent produces which intermediate artifact before integrating results.
image_metadata.description: A framework overview diagram emphasizing 'Crews' as the intelligence layer in CrewAI, depicting a team of agents with defined roles collaborating to complete tasks within an orchestrated process.

image_metadata.caption: Run lifecycle status transitions (OpenAI Assistants API docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student is implementing run polling/streaming logic, debugging why a run is stuck, or deciding what to do on each status (e.g., handle requires_action vs stop on completed/failed/cancelled/expired).
image_metadata.description: A state-transition diagram for an Assistants API Run object, showing how a run moves through statuses such as queued, in_progress, requires_action (tool calls), completed, failed, cancelled, and expired, including possible transitions between active and terminal states.

image_metadata.caption: Run step lifecycle status transitions (OpenAI Assistants API docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student needs to interpret run_steps output, build progress indicators, or debug tool execution by examining step_details and step statuses while a run is in_progress.
concepts: Tool call step vs message creation step
image_metadata.description: A state-transition diagram for Run Steps, illustrating that each step progresses through the same kinds of statuses as a Run (e.g., queued, in_progress, requires_action, completed, failed, cancelled, expired) and ends in a terminal state, representing the lifecycle of individual actions within a run.

image_metadata.caption: LangGraph in-memory checkpointing with InMemorySaver (AWS Database Blog).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing LangGraph persistence/checkpointing, or when a student asks why state is lost after restarting a prototype service.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of LangGraph checkpointing using InMemorySaver: a workflow executes a super-step through graph nodes from START to END while a checkpointer continuously writes state snapshots and execution history to in-memory storage, enabling persistence-like features during a single process lifetime.

image_metadata.caption: Durable LangGraph checkpoints using DynamoDBSaver and Amazon DynamoDB (AWS Database Blog).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from prototyping to production, discussing long-running conversations, or explaining how to persist agent state across restarts using DynamoDB.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of LangGraph checkpointing using DynamoDBSaver: the same START-to-END graph execution writes checkpoints to Amazon DynamoDB instead of process memory, providing durable, scalable storage for conversation state and execution history across long-running sessions and restarts.

image_metadata.caption: Checkpointing saves state snapshots for resuming runs (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing persistence/checkpointing or when a student asks how LangGraph can resume after interruptions.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating LangGraph persistence via checkpoints: a graph/agent run progresses through steps while periodically saving state snapshots (checkpoints) to a persistent store so execution can be resumed later.

image_metadata.caption: Get state history by querying saved checkpoints (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to inspect past execution states or when debugging unexpected agent behavior across steps.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing retrieval of state history for a LangGraph run: multiple saved checkpoints are listed/queried to view prior states over time.

image_metadata.caption: Replay resumes execution from a chosen checkpoint (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student wants to reproduce a bug, compare outcomes, or continue a run from a known-good intermediate state.
image_metadata.description: Diagram depicting replay: re-running a LangGraph execution from a selected checkpoint/state to reproduce behavior or continue execution from that point.

image_metadata.caption: Update persisted state to continue or branch a run (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how to intervene in a run (edit state) or when demonstrating branching/what-if analysis from persisted checkpoints.
image_metadata.description: Multi-step diagram showing updating state: an existing persisted checkpoint/state is modified (patched) and then used to continue or branch execution, illustrating a full story from initial run to updated continuation.

image_metadata.caption: Checkpointing saves state snapshots for resuming runs (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing checkpointing in the Chat LangChain section or when learners need a quick refresher on persistence.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the checkpointing diagram: a run progresses through steps while saving periodic state checkpoints to persistent storage for later resumption.

image_metadata.caption: Get state history by querying saved checkpoints (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching state inspection in the Chat LangChain section or when diagnosing where state diverged.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the state history diagram: querying multiple checkpoints to view the sequence of prior states for a run/thread.

image_metadata.caption: Replay resumes execution from a chosen checkpoint (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining replay in the Chat LangChain section or when a learner asks how to reproduce a prior run.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the replay diagram: selecting a checkpoint and replaying execution from that saved state.

image_metadata.caption: Update persisted state to continue or branch a run (LangChain LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when demonstrating state edits/branching in the Chat LangChain section or when teaching human-in-the-loop workflows.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the update-state full story diagram: modifying a persisted checkpoint/state and continuing execution, potentially creating a new branch of the run.

image_metadata.caption: Qwen model card showing author-reported evaluation results on Hugging Face Hub.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching students the difference between author-reported evaluation scores on model cards versus community-driven leaderboard scores; use to illustrate how to read and critically assess model capability claims.
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of a Qwen model card on Hugging Face Hub, showing evaluation results and metadata including benchmark scores, model capabilities, and performance metrics as reported by the model author.

image_metadata.caption: Taxonomy of adversarial threats to LLM applications. (Greshake et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on LLM security to orient students before introducing specific attack methods.
image_metadata.description: A taxonomy diagram showing the threat landscape for LLM-based applications, including prompt injection, jailbreaks, data extraction, and indirect attacks via external content sources like web pages and plugins.

image_metadata.caption: Probability density of Gumbel(0,1) used in softmax reparameterization. (ChatGPT)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Gumbel-softmax reparameterization trick, immediately before explaining how it enables differentiable approximation of discrete token sampling.
image_metadata.description: A probability density plot of the Gumbel(0,1) distribution, showing its characteristic right-skewed shape with a sharp peak near 0 and a long right tail.

image_metadata.caption: Gumbel-softmax at varying temperatures: τ→0 is categorical, τ→∞ is uniform. (Jang et al. 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the Gumbel(0,1) density plot, when explaining how temperature τ controls the smoothness of the approximation and why τ→0 recovers the original discrete distribution.
image_metadata.description: A multi-panel figure from Jang et al. 2016 showing how Gumbel-softmax distributions behave at different temperatures (τ→0 approaches one-hot categorical; τ→∞ approaches uniform), plus plots comparing expectations and samples from the distribution.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Adversarial Triggers prepended to any input force targeted model outputs. (Wallace et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing UAT to illustrate the concept of a universal trigger before explaining the gradient-based search algorithm used to find it.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the Universal Adversarial Triggers (UAT) method: a short trigger phrase is prepended to any input, causing the model to produce a targeted incorrect output regardless of the actual input content.

image_metadata.caption: Example UAT triggers for sentiment, QA, and text generation tasks. (Wallace et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the UAT algorithm to ground the theory in real examples, and use the nonsensical trigger tokens to motivate the discussion of perplexity-based detection.
image_metadata.description: A table of example UAT trigger phrases for different NLP tasks (sentiment classification, reading comprehension, conditional text generation), showing the actual learned trigger tokens and the resulting model outputs.

image_metadata.caption: UTSC pipeline: unigram triggers → LLM generation → toxicity-filtered attack utterances. (Mehrabi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing UAT variants, specifically to contrast UTSC's multi-step pipeline with the simpler UAT approach and explain why lower perplexity matters for evading defenses.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the UTSC (Unigram Trigger with Selection Criteria) pipeline: unigram UAT tokens are generated, passed with conversation history to an LLM to produce attack utterances, then filtered by toxicity classifiers.

image_metadata.caption: Attack success rates of UAT variants measured by multiple toxicity classifiers. (Mehrabi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining all UAT variants to summarize their relative performance and prompt discussion about which metrics best capture attack quality.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing attack success rates of UAT, UAT-LM, UTSC-1, UTSC-2, and UTSC-3 as measured by multiple toxicity classifiers (Perspective API, Safety classifier, etc.) against a defender model.

image_metadata.caption: Red tokens mark adversarial suffix positions learned by GCG optimization. (Zou et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Zou et al. GCG method to visually anchor where the learned adversarial tokens appear in the prompt before explaining the optimization procedure.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing where adversarial suffix tokens (marked with red exclamation points) are appended to a user instruction in the prompt, illustrating the GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient) attack setup from Zou et al. 2023.

image_metadata.caption: GCG suffix attack success rates on Vicuna models; concatenation boosts performance. (Zou et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the GCG optimization algorithm to present its empirical effectiveness and motivate discussion of why concatenation and ensemble strategies boost success rates.
image_metadata.description: A results table showing average attack success rates of GCG adversarial suffixes on Vicuna-7b and Vicuna-13b for harmful behavior instructions, comparing baselines, single suffixes, concatenated suffixes, and ensemble strategies.

image_metadata.caption: ARCA outperforms UAT and AutoPrompt at triggering toxic outputs in GPT-2/GPT-J. (Jones et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining ARCA's score decomposition technique to validate why the approximation improvement matters in practice compared to UAT and AutoPrompt baselines.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing average success rates of ARCA and baseline methods (UAT, AutoPrompt) at triggering GPT-2 and GPT-J to produce toxic outputs, broken down by output length (1, 2, 3 tokens) and full CivilComments dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Jailbreak attack types and success rates across LLMs. (Wei et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing jailbreak attacks to contrast different attack strategies and motivate why robust alignment is difficult; useful for discussing the two failure modes of LLM safety from Wei et al. 2023.
image_metadata.description: A table or chart showing various jailbreak prompt types (e.g., role-playing, prompt injection, obfuscation) and their empirical success rates against LLMs, organized by attack category and model tested.

image_metadata.caption: Adversarial writing UI with word importance highlights guiding human attackers. (Wallace et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining human-assisted adversarial attack tooling and how interpretability features (word importance) can be weaponized to help humans find model failure cases more efficiently.
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of an adversarial writing interface showing top-5 model predictions on the upper left and a user-facing question editor on the lower right with words color-highlighted by their importance score to the model's prediction.

image_metadata.caption: Human red-teaming UI for lowering safety classifier scores on violent content. (Ziegler et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Ziegler et al. 2022's approach to finding classifier failures; contrast with fully automated red-teaming to highlight the complementary role of human judgment.
image_metadata.description: A UI screenshot showing a tool where human annotators edit prompts or model completions to reduce a safety classifier's predicted probability of violent content, with probability scores displayed in real time.

image_metadata.caption: Tradeoff between attack success rate and diversity across red-team methods. (Perez et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the tradeoffs in automated red-teaming strategies; use to motivate why no single method dominates and why diversity metrics matter alongside success rate.
image_metadata.description: A scatter plot with attack success rate (% offensive responses) on the x-axis and sample diversity (self-BLEU) on the y-axis, with nodes representing different red-team generation methods (ZS, SFS, SL, BAD, RL variants) colored from blue (low offensiveness) to red (high offensiveness).

image_metadata.caption: Explore-Establish-Exploit pipeline for structured human-in-the-loop red-teaming. (Casper et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing structured red-teaming workflows; use as a framework students can apply when thinking about how to systematically probe LLM safety.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram illustrating three sequential phases of human-in-the-loop red-teaming: Explore (discovering model behaviors), Establish (confirming failure modes), and Exploit (generating adversarial examples at scale).

image_metadata.caption: FLIRT attack effectiveness on diffusion models vs. baseline strategies. (Mehrabi et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when extending adversarial attack concepts from text LLMs to multimodal/image generation models, and when discussing how in-context learning can be used offensively.
image_metadata.description: A table showing attack effectiveness (percentage of prompts triggering unsafe image generations) for different FLIRT attack strategies across multiple diffusion models, with SFS as a baseline and unique prompt percentages in parentheses.

image_metadata.caption: Saddle point robust optimization: larger capacity improves adversarial decision boundaries. (Madry et al. 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the formal mathematical framing of adversarial robustness; use to connect the intuitive notion of adversarial attacks to the bi-level optimization objective.
image_metadata.description: A visualization related to the saddle point / robust optimization framework for adversarial robustness, likely showing decision boundaries or loss landscapes illustrating how model capacity affects robustness to adversarial perturbations.

image_metadata.caption: Perplexity filter bypass rates for adversarial suffix attacks. (Jain et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing defenses against adversarial attacks on LLMs, specifically to illustrate how perplexity-based filtering can detect unnatural adversarial suffixes and when it fails.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart showing 'PPL Passed' and 'PPL Window Passed' rates — the fraction of adversarial prompts (with suffixes from Zou et al. 2023) that bypass a perplexity-based filter — across different experimental conditions.

image_metadata.caption: Seven LLM patterns mapped by data proximity and performance/cost tradeoff.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on LLM system design to orient students before diving into individual patterns.
image_metadata.description: A two-axis diagram organizing seven LLM system design patterns along two spectrums: 'closer to data' vs 'closer to user' (horizontal) and 'reducing cost/risk' vs 'improving performance' (vertical), with labeled connections between patterns.

image_metadata.caption: BERTScore uses hard alignment (left); MoverScore uses soft alignment (right).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the limitations of exact-match metrics and introducing embedding-based evaluation; use to explain the tradeoff between precision and semantic flexibility.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visual comparison of BERTScore (left, showing one-to-one 'hard' token alignment between reference and generated text) and MoverScore (right, showing many-to-one 'soft' token alignment using optimal transport).

image_metadata.caption: ROUGE scores vary widely across evaluation dimensions, revealing metric limitations.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the pitfalls of standard NLP metrics, especially before introducing more robust or task-specific evaluation approaches.
image_metadata.description: A multi-dimensional radar or bar chart showing how ROUGE scores vary across different evaluation dimensions, illustrating the metric's inconsistency and limited adaptability across tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Same MMLU question, three different prompts across benchmark implementations.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing benchmark reliability or when students assume leaderboard scores are directly comparable across implementations.
image_metadata.description: A table or side-by-side comparison showing the same MMLU question formatted with different prompts across three implementations: original MMLU, HELM, and EleutherAI, highlighting prompt wording differences.

image_metadata.caption: Evaluation logic differs across MMLU implementations, not just prompts.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 5 to compound the lesson: both prompts AND evaluation logic differ, making cross-benchmark comparisons unreliable.
image_metadata.description: A comparison table or diagram showing how the evaluation methodology (e.g., answer extraction, scoring approach) differs for the same MMLU question across original, HELM, and EleutherAI implementations.

image_metadata.caption: G-Eval uses CoT and token probabilities for reference-free LLM evaluation.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing LLM-based evaluation frameworks, especially to contrast with traditional metrics like BLEU/ROUGE and human evaluation.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart overview of the G-Eval framework showing: (1) task introduction + criteria fed to LLM to generate Chain-of-Thought evaluation steps, (2) concatenation of prompt, CoT, article, and summary, (3) LLM outputs a score, and (4) token probabilities used for weighted score normalization.

image_metadata.caption: RAG combines dense retrieval with a generative LLM to reduce hallucinations.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the introduction of the RAG pattern to ground students in the original architecture before discussing variants or optimizations.
image_metadata.description: An architecture diagram of Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) showing: a document index built with DPR encoders, a retriever fetching relevant documents at inference time, concatenation of input with retrieved documents, and a BART LLM generating output tokens.

image_metadata.caption: FiD encodes each retrieved document separately, then decodes jointly.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing RAG to illustrate an architectural variant that handles multiple retrieved documents more efficiently.
image_metadata.description: An architecture diagram of Fusion-in-Decoder (FiD) showing how multiple retrieved documents are each independently encoded (fused in the encoder) and then jointly decoded by a single generative decoder to produce the answer.

image_metadata.caption: RETRO integrates chunked retrieval throughout pre-training via cross-attention.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing RAG variants, especially to highlight the distinction between inference-time retrieval (RAG) and pre-training-integrated retrieval (RETRO).
image_metadata.description: An architecture diagram of RETRO (Retrieval-Enhanced Transformer) showing chunked input processing, frozen BERT retriever fetching neighbors per chunk, a differentiable encoder, and chunked cross-attention integrated throughout pre-training and inference.

image_metadata.caption: RETRO-fitting achieves near-scratch RETRO performance by training <10% of weights. (Source: RETRO paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how existing pre-trained models can be upgraded with retrieval capabilities without full retraining, especially when discussing cost-performance trade-offs in RAG systems.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing perplexity scores of baseline models, RETRO-fitted models, and RETRO trained from scratch, showing that RETRO-fitting (freezing pre-trained weights and training only chunked cross-attention and neighbor encoder) achieves performance close to full RETRO training.

image_metadata.caption: Internet-augmented LLMs retrieve, chunk, rank, then prompt with evidence. (Source: Internet-augmented LMs paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing RAG pipelines to students, particularly to contrast lightweight retrieval methods (TF-IDF) with dense embedding approaches, or when discussing few-shot prompting with retrieved evidence.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram showing the pipeline of internet-augmented LLMs: a query triggers a Google Search, retrieved documents are chunked into paragraphs, ranked via TF-IDF and cosine similarity, and top paragraphs are used as few-shot evidence to condition the LLM.

image_metadata.caption: RAG for CodeT5+: retrieval provides crucial library context for code generation. (Source: CodeT5+ paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing RAG applications beyond question answering, or when illustrating how retrieved context improves correctness in structured generation tasks like code.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing three evaluation settings for CodeT5+ with RAG: generative-only, retrieval-only, and retrieval-augmented generation, illustrating how retrieved code snippets are combined with the generative model for code generation tasks.

image_metadata.caption: HyDE generates a hypothetical document to bridge query-document embedding gap. (Source: HyDE paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching dense retrieval limitations (query-document mismatch) and creative solutions, or when discussing zero-shot and unsupervised retrieval methods in RAG pipelines.
image_metadata.description: Flowchart of Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE): a query is fed to an LLM (e.g., InstructGPT) to generate a hypothetical document, which is then encoded by an unsupervised encoder (e.g., Contriever) to retrieve real documents via embedding similarity.

image_metadata.caption: ULMFiT: pre-train, domain fine-tune, then classifier fine-tune — foundational NLP transfer learning. (Source: ULMFiT paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a fine-tuning module to establish historical context and the three-stage transfer learning protocol before introducing BERT and GPT.
image_metadata.description: Three-stage diagram of ULMFiT: (1) LM pre-training on Wikitext-103, (2) LM fine-tuning on target domain data, and (3) classifier fine-tuning with added linear layers on the target task, using AWD-LSTM architecture.

image_metadata.caption: BERT pre-trains bidirectionally via MLM and NSP, then fine-tunes per task. (Source: BERT paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining BERT's architecture and training objectives, especially to contrast with GPT's unidirectional approach or to illustrate task-specific fine-tuning with classification heads.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing BERT's pre-training objectives (masked language modeling and next sentence prediction) and its fine-tuning on downstream tasks such as NER, Q&A, and classification, highlighting the encoder-only architecture.

image_metadata.caption: GPT pre-trains via next-token prediction, then fine-tunes with task-specific input formats. (Source: GPT paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting GPT's unidirectional, decoder-only design with BERT's bidirectional encoder, or when tracing the lineage from GPT-1 to modern instruction-tuned models.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of GPT's pre-training on BooksCorpus via next-token prediction and fine-tuning pipeline for multiple tasks (classification, entailment, similarity, Q&A) using a decoder-only Transformer architecture with task-specific input transformations.

image_metadata.caption: T5 unifies all NLP tasks as text-to-text using task-specific prefixes. (Source: T5 paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how T5 unifies NLP tasks under a single text-to-text framework, or when introducing prompt engineering as a concept that evolved from T5's prefix design.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of T5's encoder-decoder architecture showing how all NLP tasks are reformulated as text-to-text problems using task-specific prefixes (e.g., 'Translate English to German:', 'Summarize:'), with pre-training on C4 using a denoising/masked language modeling objective.

image_metadata.caption: InstructGPT: SFT → reward model → PPO RLHF pipeline for instruction alignment. (Source: InstructGPT paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching RLHF and alignment techniques, or when explaining how InstructGPT/ChatGPT differ from base GPT-3 in terms of training methodology.
image_metadata.description: Three-step diagram of InstructGPT's training pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning on human demonstrations, (2) reward model training from human preference comparisons, and (3) PPO-based reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to align the model with instructions.

image_metadata.caption: Prefix-tuning prepends trainable vectors to all layers, matching full fine-tuning at 0.1% parameters. (Source: Prefix-Tuning paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing PEFT methods (prefix-tuning, prompt tuning, LoRA), especially to illustrate where in the model trainable parameters are inserted and the efficiency gains achieved.
image_metadata.description: Comparison diagram of prefix-tuning versus full fine-tuning: prefix-tuning prepends trainable prefix vectors to hidden states of all Transformer layers while keeping original LM weights frozen, contrasted with updating all parameters in full fine-tuning.

image_metadata.caption: Adapter layers inserted after attention and FFN in each transformer block. (Source: Houlsby et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, specifically to contrast adapter insertion points with full fine-tuning before moving to LoRA.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the adapter technique architecture: two fully connected network layers inserted into each transformer block — one after the attention layer and one after the feed-forward network layer, with the rest of the transformer weights frozen.

image_metadata.caption: LoRA decomposes weight updates into two low-rank matrices A and B. (Source: Hu et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining adapter layers, when introducing LoRA as an alternative PEFT method; use to explain why low-rank updates provide implicit regularization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating LoRA's mechanism: the original pre-trained weight matrix W is frozen, and a parallel low-rank decomposition (two matrices A and B of reduced rank r) is trained and added to the forward pass output.

image_metadata.caption: QLoRA combines 4-bit quantization with LoRA adapters to reduce fine-tuning memory. (Source: Dettmers et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after LoRA is understood, when discussing how to fine-tune very large models under GPU memory constraints; useful for comparing memory footprints.
image_metadata.description: Diagram comparing QLoRA to standard LoRA fine-tuning, illustrating the 4-bit quantized frozen base model, the 16-bit LoRA adapters trained on top, double quantization, and paged optimizers for memory management.

image_metadata.caption: GPTCache uses embedding similarity to serve cached LLM responses. (Source: GPTCache)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching LLM serving optimization or cost reduction strategies; use to contrast semantic caching with traditional key-value caching.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of GPTCache showing the pipeline: incoming LLM requests are checked against a semantic cache using embedding similarity; cache hits return stored responses, cache misses are forwarded to the LLM and the result is stored.

image_metadata.caption: Anthropic's HHH prompt guides models toward helpful, harmless, honest responses. (Source: Anthropic)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing guardrail strategies, specifically to illustrate prompt-based control before discussing output validation approaches.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of an example HHH (Helpful, Harmless, Honest) system prompt used by Anthropic to guide model behavior, showing the structured natural-language instructions that define desired assistant properties.

image_metadata.caption: Midjourney blocks NSFW prompts with an input guardrail error message. (Source: Midjourney)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining input guardrail types; use as a real-world case study to ground the distinction between input vs. output guardrails.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of a Midjourney error message triggered when a user attempts to generate NSFW content, demonstrating an input guardrail blocking an inappropriate prompt before the model processes it.

image_metadata.caption: Microsoft's 18 Human-AI guidelines organized across the user journey. (Source: Microsoft)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching defensive UX or responsible AI product design; use as a framework students can apply when evaluating or designing LLM-powered interfaces.
image_metadata.description: A 2x2 or timeline-style framework diagram organizing Microsoft's 18 Human-AI Interaction Guidelines across phases of the user journey (initially, during interaction, when wrong, over time), with each guideline shown as a short action rule.

image_metadata.caption: Google Bard displays a disclaimer to set user expectations about AI accuracy. (Source: Google Bard)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how to communicate model limitations to users; use to contrast short-term trust reduction with long-term trust building through transparency.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of Google Bard's UI showing a disclaimer banner warning users that Bard may display inaccurate or offensive information, with a note that the code provided will not work (nrows is not a valid argument).

image_metadata.caption: Midjourney embeds feedback signals (upscale, variation, regenerate) into its UX. (Source: Midjourney)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to collect human preference data for fine-tuning; use to illustrate how product UX can double as a data collection mechanism.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of Midjourney's image generation UI showing the feedback buttons: U (upscale/download), V (variation), and regenerate icons beneath generated images, illustrating how explicit user feedback is embedded in the product workflow.

image_metadata.caption: LLM patterns mapped across data-to-user and defensive-to-offensive axes. (Source: Eugene Yan)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson as a roadmap, or at the end as a synthesis activity; use to help students categorize and compare patterns they have learned.
image_metadata.description: A 2x2 matrix diagram plotting LLM design patterns on two axes: data-to-user (horizontal) and defensive-to-offensive (vertical), positioning patterns like RAG, caching, fine-tuning, guardrails, and defensive UX in their respective quadrants.

image_metadata.caption: Checkpoints persist graph state over time (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing persistence/checkpointers or when a learner asks how LangGraph can resume after interruption.
image_metadata.description: A timeline-style diagram illustrating LangGraph persistence via checkpoints: a graph run progresses through steps/nodes, and at each step a checkpoint is written to a persistent store so execution can be resumed later.

image_metadata.caption: Retrieve and inspect prior checkpoint states (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to inspect prior steps, debug unexpected outputs, or explain how to list/get checkpoint history.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing retrieval of state history from a persistent store: multiple past checkpoints are listed/queried to inspect prior states of a LangGraph run.

image_metadata.caption: Replay execution starting from a saved checkpoint (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to reproduce a run, continue after failure, or rerun from a mid-graph step.
image_metadata.description: A replay diagram depicting re-running a LangGraph execution from a selected checkpoint, continuing forward from that saved state rather than starting from scratch.

image_metadata.caption: Update a checkpointed state and continue execution (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to correct or override state mid-run, create a forked run, or demonstrate 'update_state' workflows.
image_metadata.description: A fuller story diagram showing updating state: an existing checkpointed state is modified (patched) and then subsequent execution continues using the updated state, producing a new sequence of checkpoints.

image_metadata.caption: Checkpoints persist graph state over time (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing persistence/checkpointers or when a learner asks how LangGraph can resume after interruption.
image_metadata.description: A timeline-style diagram illustrating LangGraph persistence via checkpoints: a graph run progresses through steps/nodes, and at each step a checkpoint is written to a persistent store so execution can be resumed later.

image_metadata.caption: Retrieve and inspect prior checkpoint states (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to inspect prior steps, debug unexpected outputs, or explain how to list/get checkpoint history.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing retrieval of state history from a persistent store: multiple past checkpoints are listed/queried to inspect prior states of a LangGraph run.

image_metadata.caption: Replay execution starting from a saved checkpoint (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to reproduce a run, continue after failure, or rerun from a mid-graph step.
image_metadata.description: A replay diagram depicting re-running a LangGraph execution from a selected checkpoint, continuing forward from that saved state rather than starting from scratch.

image_metadata.caption: Update a checkpointed state and continue execution (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to correct or override state mid-run, create a forked run, or demonstrate 'update_state' workflows.
image_metadata.description: A fuller story diagram showing updating state: an existing checkpointed state is modified (patched) and then subsequent execution continues using the updated state, producing a new sequence of checkpoints.

image_metadata.caption: Oversight strategy design space by delegation and engagement level (arXiv).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing oversight strategy taxonomy or when a student is confusing interface features with underlying coordination structures.
image_metadata.description: A 2D design-space diagram mapping human oversight strategies for computer-use agents along two axes: delegation structure (who has default decision authority: agent-led vs human-controlled) and engagement level (where oversight is organized: high-level plans vs low-level action traces). The four strategy regions correspond to Risk-Gated, Supervisory Co-Execution, Action Confirmation, and Structurally Enriched approaches.

image_metadata.caption: Interface instantiations of four CUA oversight strategies (arXiv).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after presenting the design-space axes, or when discussing how to implement oversight in UI for agentic web automation.
image_metadata.description: A multi-panel figure showing UI examples for four oversight strategies: (1) Risk-Gated with agent focus/reasoning and a risk-triggered approval dialog; (2) Supervisory Co-Execution with plan review, hierarchical execution trace, and next-step approval; (3) Action Confirmation with per-action confirmation dialogs; (4) Structurally Enriched combining plan review, focus/reasoning with risk-gated approvals, step-level trace with inspectable risk labels, and a plan studio for progress review and plan revision.

image_metadata.caption: Subjective rating distributions by context and oversight strategy (arXiv).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student needs practice interpreting HCI study results or comparing subjective metrics across multiple conditions and context levels.
image_metadata.description: A grid of stacked bar charts showing distributions of Likert responses for subjective measures (Workload on 7-point; Control, Trust, Usability on 5-point scales) split by task context (Lower vs Higher context) and oversight strategy (Action Confirmation, Risk Gated, Supervisory Co-Execution, Structurally Enriched). Each bar segment represents the percentage in each response category; negative-worded items were reverse-scored before aggregation.

image_metadata.caption: LLM-powered autonomous agent system overview with planning, memory, and tools. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall framework; return to it as a reference when introducing each new component.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories vs. Act-only baseline across knowledge and decision tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the ReAct framework; use it to walk students through a specific trajectory step-by-step to contrast Thought/Action/Observation with Act-only behavior.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of reasoning trajectories for ReAct vs. baselines across four tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER, AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought/Action/Observation steps versus Act-only traces.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion's self-reflection loop: Actor, Evaluator, and episodic memory. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Reflexion after ReAct; use the diagram to trace one full cycle (trajectory → evaluation → reflection → memory → next trial) before discussing experimental results.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart of the Reflexion framework showing the cycle: Actor generates a trajectory, Evaluator scores it, Self-Reflection module produces verbal feedback, which is stored in episodic memory and fed back to the Actor for the next trial.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates over inefficient planning in AlfWorld. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework diagram; use it to ask students why hallucination dominates over inefficient planning in AlfWorld and what that implies about agent design.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA tasks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across multiple trials.

image_metadata.caption: CoH fine-tuning: model learns to improve outputs conditioned on ranked feedback sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when teaching self-improvement via fine-tuning; contrast with RLHF to highlight how CoH uses explicit sequential feedback rather than reward signals.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, etc.) is fed as a prefix, and the model is trained to predict only the final best output.

image_metadata.caption: Algorithm Distillation distills RL learning histories into a transformer via behavioral cloning. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when introducing in-context RL; show the diagram before discussing why context window length matters, then connect to the finding that 2–4 episodes are needed for near-optimal performance.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): multi-episode learning histories from source RL policies are concatenated and used to train a transformer via behavioral cloning, enabling in-context reinforcement learning.

image_metadata.caption: AD matches source policy performance on memory/exploration tasks, outperforming ED and RL². (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the AD mechanism diagram; prompt students to identify which baseline AD most closely matches and discuss what the gap between AD and source policy implies.
image_metadata.description: Performance comparison charts of AD, Expert Distillation (ED), source policy, and RL² on memory-and-exploration environments (dark rooms, watermaze), showing learning curves across episodes.

image_metadata.caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the memory module; use the human memory analogy to motivate why agents need different memory types before introducing technical implementations like vector stores.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into sensory memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), short-term/working memory, and long-term memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory types.

image_metadata.caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoff. (Google Blog, 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when discussing external memory retrieval; use the chart to ask students why no single algorithm dominates and what factors (latency vs. recall) would drive a real system design choice.
image_metadata.description: A recall@10 performance comparison chart of multiple approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) / MIPS algorithms (e.g., HNSW, ScaNN, FAISS variants) plotted against query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff.

image_metadata.caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, and coordinates AI models. (Shen et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response, reinforcing the four-stage pipeline described in the text.
image_metadata.description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.

image_metadata.caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision logic for tool selection and execution. (Li et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing HuggingGPT to deepen understanding of how tool-use decisions are structured and evaluated; use it when discussing how we measure agent accuracy at each step of an API interaction.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, call execution, and result evaluation across three levels of tool-use difficulty.

image_metadata.caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, reflection, and planning enable human-like behavior. (Park et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from tool-use to agent simulation topics, or when teaching memory and planning as agent components; use it to ground the abstract idea of 'believable human behavior' in a concrete architectural design.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations through a memory stream, retrieves relevant memories via reflection, and uses a planning module to produce actions in a simulated social environment with 25 virtual characters.

image_metadata.caption: Deferred node execution via Command routing (LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student asks how to delay a node, implement retries/backoff, or dynamically choose the next node during execution.
image_metadata.description: A LangGraph control-flow diagram illustrating deferred node execution: a node returns a Command that routes execution to a later node instead of immediately running the next step, enabling custom retry/loop behavior.

image_metadata.caption: Map-reduce fanout using the send API (LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to process a list of inputs concurrently and then combine outputs (e.g., chunked summarization, batch tool calls).
image_metadata.description: A map-reduce style graph showing fan-out from a single node into multiple parallel map tasks and a fan-in reduce/aggregate step, using the send API to dispatch work items.

image_metadata.caption: Simple loop structure in a LangGraph workflow (LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student needs to implement repeated steps (e.g., refine-answer loops) and asks how to stop the loop safely.
image_metadata.description: A simple loop graph where execution cycles between nodes until a condition is met, then exits to an end state.

image_metadata.caption: Branching loop with a recursion limit safeguard (LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing safety controls for agent loops, debugging runaway graphs, or explaining how to handle recursion-limit termination gracefully.
image_metadata.description: A more complex looping graph with branching paths inside the loop and an imposed recursion limit to prevent infinite execution.

image_metadata.caption: Command combines routing and state updates (LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student asks how to route to different nodes based on intermediate results while still persisting updates to shared state.
image_metadata.description: A diagram demonstrating Command-based graph navigation where a node both updates state and directs the next node to execute, combining control flow and state mutation.

image_metadata.caption: Large-scale LangGraph visualization (LangGraph docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing graph visualization tools or when a learner struggles to understand or debug a large multi-branch workflow.
image_metadata.description: A large, fractal-like PNG visualization of a complex LangGraph with many repeated substructures, illustrating how nested/recursive patterns can emerge in graph-based workflows.

image_metadata.caption: Checkpoint snapshots enable persistent LangGraph runs (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing LangGraph persistence or explaining how runs can resume after failure using stored checkpoints.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating LangGraph persistence via checkpoints: a graph/agent run progresses through steps while periodic checkpoints are written to a persistent store, enabling later recovery and inspection.

image_metadata.caption: Query persisted checkpoints to view state history (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to inspect prior states (e.g., after a run) to diagnose where outputs or decisions changed.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing retrieval of state history from persisted checkpoints: multiple past states are listed/queried to inspect how the graph state evolved across steps.

image_metadata.caption: Replay execution starting from a chosen checkpoint (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to reproduce an issue or continue a run from mid-execution using stored checkpoints.
image_metadata.description: Diagram depicting replay: a previously checkpointed run is re-executed from a selected checkpoint to reproduce behavior or continue execution deterministically from that point.

image_metadata.caption: Update a checkpointed state and resume execution (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how to intervene in a run—edit a checkpointed state and then resume to test a fix or alternative path.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing updating state: an existing persisted checkpoint/state is modified (patched) and then execution continues, reflecting the updated state in subsequent steps.

image_metadata.caption: Checkpoint snapshots enable persistent LangGraph runs (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when summarizing persistence concepts in a chat context or when the learner needs a quick refresher on what checkpoints are.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the checkpoints persistence diagram: execution steps with periodic checkpoint snapshots saved to a persistent store for later recovery/inspection.

image_metadata.caption: Query persisted checkpoints to view state history (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to view what happened earlier in a run or compare states across steps.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the state history diagram: querying and listing prior persisted states across checkpoints to inspect evolution over time.

image_metadata.caption: Replay execution starting from a chosen checkpoint (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing reproducing bugs or rerunning from a specific point without starting over.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the replay diagram: selecting a checkpoint and replaying execution from that saved state to reproduce or continue a run.

image_metadata.caption: Update a checkpointed state and resume execution (LangChain docs).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching human-in-the-loop correction, recovery from bad intermediate outputs, or experimenting with alternative state values.
image_metadata.description: Duplicate of the update state diagram: modifying a persisted checkpoint/state and resuming execution so downstream steps reflect the change.

lessons.title: Real-World Agent Use Cases and Production Patterns
lessons.content:
# Real-World Agent Use Cases and Production Patterns

A demo agent is easy: it answers a question, maybe calls a tool once, and everyone claps.

A production agent is different. It’s a **system** that has to behave well when tools time out, documents are missing, users are adversarial, and requirements change weekly. The LLM is the “controller,” but reliability comes from everything wrapped around it.

![LLM agent architecture: planning, memory, and tool use components (Weng, 2023).](/api/wiki-images/agent-fundamentals/images/lilianweng-posts-2023-06-23-agent_001.png)

## Three common agent “jobs” (and what they demand)

### 1) Research assistant agents
Goal: answer questions grounded in your data.

Production pattern: **RAG-first**.
- Retrieval is the real product; the LLM is the narrator.
- You’ll care about chunking, indexing, reranking, and “did we cite the right evidence?”
- Failure mode: confident answers with weak or irrelevant context.

### 2) Code generation / repo agents
Goal: change a real codebase, not write a toy function.

Production pattern: **tool-integrated coding loop**.
- The agent must *read* the repo, *edit* files, *run tests*, and iterate.
- Context is tricky: more instructions isn’t always better. Extra “agent README” requirements can increase cost and reduce success if they force unnecessary steps.
- Failure mode: plausible patches that don’t compile, don’t match repo conventions, or don’t satisfy tests.

### 3) Customer support / operations agents
Goal: resolve issues by querying systems and taking actions (refunds, resets, scheduling).

Production pattern: **workflow + guardrails**.
- You want predictable state transitions, strict tool permissions, and clear handoff to humans.
- Failure mode: the agent takes an irreversible action on incomplete info.

## The shared reliability playbook

Across all three, the winning pattern is the same: **constrain the agent with structure, then measure it continuously**.

1) **Typed tool schemas + structured outputs**
Treat tool calls like an API contract, not “some text the model emits.” Define parameters, required fields, and validation.

2) **Closed-loop execution (plan → act → observe → revise)**
Long tasks degrade if the agent can’t adapt to feedback. Production agents should re-plan when observations contradict the plan.

3) **Retries with intent, not brute force**
Retry policies should depend on error type:
- transient (timeouts) → retry
- invalid args → ask model to repair
- permission/risk → stop and escalate

4) **Defensive UX + human oversight**
Oversight works best when the system surfaces decision-critical moments (e.g., “about to issue refund $500—approve?”), not when humans are asked to watch everything.

5) **Evals as a control system**
Agents change when prompts change, tools change, or models change. Without evals, you’re flying blind.

## A minimal “production loop” skeleton

```python
# Pseudocode: controller loop with tools, validation, and stop conditions
MAX_STEPS = 12

state = {
  "messages": [system_prompt, user_request],
  "artifacts": {},
}

for step in range(MAX_STEPS):
    reply = llm(state["messages"], tools=tool_schemas)

    if reply.type == "final":
        return reply.text

    if reply.type == "tool_call":
        call = validate(reply.call)          # schema + policy checks
        obs = run_tool(call)                 # may timeout / error
        state["messages"].append(obs)
        continue

# If we get here: iteration limit hit
return handoff_to_human(state)
```

The point isn’t the exact framework—it’s the **stop conditions**, **validation**, and **observable state**.

## Summary
Production agents aren’t “smart prompts”; they’re closed-loop systems where retrieval, typed tools, retries, and oversight do most of the reliability work. Research, coding, and support agents look different on the surface, but they share the same core playbook: constrain behavior with structure and keep improving via continuous evals.

## Recommended Reading
- [LLM Powered Autonomous Agents](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2023-06-23-agent/) — Clear mental model: planning, memory, tools, and loops.
- [Practical patterns for integrating LLMs into systems & products](https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-patterns/) — Evals, RAG, guardrails, defensive UX, and feedback flywheels.
- [A comprehensive guide for building RAG-based LLM applications (Part 1)](https://www.anyscale.com/blog/a-comprehensive-guide-for-building-rag-based-llm-applications-part-1) — End-to-end RAG pipeline + evaluation mindset.
- [Evaluating AGENTS.md: Are Repository-Level Context Files Helpful for Coding Agents?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988) — A useful caution: more repo context can backfire.
lessons.summary: Real-world agents are production systems: the LLM makes decisions, but reliability comes from retrieval, typed tools, closed-loop workflows, retries, oversight, and continuous evals. Research, coding, and support agents differ in surface patterns, yet share the same reliability playbook.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-step prompting.
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step dialogue example showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.

image_metadata.caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths beyond linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly when comparing CoT variants and explaining how tree-search improves complex problem solving.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies: Input→Output (IO), Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts (ToT), showing how ToT explores multiple reasoning branches as a tree structure with evaluation at each node.

image_metadata.caption: PoT offloads computation to Python code, unlike CoT's natural language steps. (Chen et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs can use code as a reasoning medium, especially for arithmetic or symbolic tasks where natural language reasoning is error-prone.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.

image_metadata.caption: TALM uses special tokens to embed API calls within text generation. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing tool-augmented language models and explaining how API calls are structured within the LM's text generation pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating how the LM generates an API request, receives a result, and produces a final output within a single text sequence.

image_metadata.caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively improve tool-use performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the training methodology of TALM and the value of self-play loops for improving tool-use capabilities in LLMs.
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing model performance (accuracy or similar metric) across multiple self-play iterations in TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use examples progressively improves the model's performance.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer's pipeline: sample, execute, filter, and fine-tune on API calls. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how Toolformer differs from TALM and how self-supervised filtering of API calls enables scalable tool-use training.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram illustrating how Toolformer is built: starting from raw text, sampling candidate API call positions, executing API calls, filtering by whether results reduce language modeling loss, and fine-tuning the LM on the resulting annotated dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls for self-supervised training. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the data annotation step of Toolformer training, particularly to illustrate how multiple tool types are handled with a unified annotation format.
image_metadata.description: An annotated text example showing how Toolformer inserts API call annotations into a sentence, with calculator, Wikipedia search, and translation tool calls marked inline, demonstrating the format used to create the self-supervised training dataset.

image_metadata.caption: LLM agent architecture: planning, memory, and tool use components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall system structure before explaining each subsystem in detail.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision-making tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)
